text: A one-room museum, admission two coins, curated by a man who calls every exhibit 'priceless' while pricing them. The centerpiece plaque tells an old story: "{rumor}"

outcomes.text: Half the exhibits are mislabeled and one is visibly breathing. Still, you leave knowing things. Some of them true.
You correct a plaque date out loud. The curator makes you an honorary docent on the spot, which pays in exposure and one actual coin.

The rain has driven half the market inside, and the curator is charging them all double for 'weather admission.' You shelter among the exhibits with the damp crowd and learn more from their arguing than from any of the plaques.

The curator recognizes you from the broadsheets and waives the fee, on the condition that you stand beside the breathing exhibit while he letters 'HERO VISITS COLLECTION' for the window. Fame: worth two coins a day, apparently.

choices.text: Sell him something from your pack

outcomes.text: You present the rock with a straight face and three invented dynasties of provenance. He pays museum prices. There is now a plinth for it.
'That one's on loan,' says the curator, not looking. The exhibit winks at you. Some knowledge costs nothing and weighs plenty.

text: A goblin in a tiny formal doublet stands on the bar of {site}, reading from a scroll: 'PEACE PROPOSAL, ARTICLE ONE: EVERYONE CALM DOWN.' Nobody is not calm, which is throwing off his rhythm.

outcomes.text: Emboldened, he reads all nine articles. Article seven is just the word 'SOUP.' The tavern ratifies it unanimously. History is made, probably.

With the war this close, nobody laughs. The goblin reads all nine articles into a silence that actually listens, and the whole tavern drinks to Article One like it might work. For one evening, in one room, everyone calms down.

success.text: Your amendment ('soup FOR ALL') passes by acclamation. The goblin appoints you deputy envoy and pays you from the diplomacy budget, which is a sock of coins.

fail.text: You are ruled out of order by a goblin in a doublet, which stings more than you'd think. The tavern sides with the doublet.

outcomes.text: He checks the scroll. It doesn't say. He checks his doublet. Nothing. The existential crisis that follows requires two ales, which you buy out of pity and curiosity.
'The rock,' he whispers. You know the one. You leave before jurisdiction attaches.

text: Half-buried in the wall: a slab of sky-metal with a cracked glass face. When you approach, it flickers alive: 'WELCOME, CUSTOMER. YOUR WAIT TIME IS: 847 YEARS. YOUR CALL IS IMPORTANT TO US.'

outcomes.text: After ten minutes of crackling music, it dispenses a complimentary credit chip 'for your patience.' The queue does not advance.
The hold music is oddly beautiful. You leave humming a song written by a corporation that died before your grandmother was born.

success.text: The terminal PANICS. Compensation protocols engage. Credits spill from a slot like a slot machine admitting fault.

fail.text: 'YOUR FEEDBACK MATTERS,' it says, and adds 200 years to your wait time out of spite.

success.text: Inside: sky-metal filaments worth good coin, and a note from a previous scavenger: 'sorry. me too.'

fail.text: The terminal defends itself with a burst of static and a threatening survey. Your hair will be ambitious for days.

outcomes.text: The slot swallows the chip with the satisfaction of a machine finally doing its actual job. 'BALANCE TRANSFERRED. THANK YOU FOR CHOOSING US. YOU HAD NO CHOICE.' Coins of several dead currencies rattle from a hatch, most still legal somewhere.
'CHIP EXPIRED 763 YEARS AGO,' it announces, keeping the chip. 'A LATE FEE HAS BEEN ASSESSED.' You have somehow left this transaction owing money to a wall.

text: Through the trees comes a procession in gray robes, chanting in a tongue that sounds like a cathedral apologizing. They carry an enormous bell on poles. The bell is cracked. They seem very proud of it.

outcomes.text: You carry a pole for an hour. The chant gets into your bones in a good way. At parting, they anoint your forehead with something that smells like rain.

Snow muffles everything except the chant and the slow creak of the cracked bell. Forty gray hoods going white, and you among them, breath keeping time. When they anoint your forehead at parting, the oil is somehow warm.

Halfway through the second chant, the eldest monk stops, sniffs, and looks at you the way a plumber looks at a wall. The procession forms a circle without discussion. Whatever they chant next has corners on it, and when it ends, something that has been riding you for miles is simply gone.
'The crack IS the relic,' explains a monk, delighted someone asked. The theology takes forty minutes. You understand none of it and feel better anyway.
Wrong question. The procession stops. Forty hooded faces turn. You apologize to the bell directly, which turns out to be correct protocol.
The horror on their faces teaches you more about faith than any sermon. They pay you to NOT fix it, which is the strangest gold you've ever earned.

text: An old soldier sits on a milestone, medals pinned to a coat two wars out of date. 'I served under {vet},' he says to no one, or to you, or to the road. 'Nobody remembers the siege. I remember the siege.'

choices.text: Sit and hear the whole siege

outcomes.text: Two hours. Mud, soup shortages, a heroic goat. By the end you remember the siege too, which is the entire point. He stands straighter when you leave.

With a war on THIS road NOW, the old man's stories stop being stories. He sketches the fortifications in the dust, marks where supply lines break, and tells you which of it still applies. All of it, as it turns out.
He sells you the third-shiniest one and invents its story fresh for the occasion. Both of you know. Neither of you says.
He refuses your coin and PINS the medal on you instead. 'For listening.' You are now decorated for a war you weren't born for.

success.text: You improvise a company, a ridge, and a sergeant with a memorable mustache. He weeps, embraces you, and splits his pension ale money with a fellow veteran.

fail.text: He asks one question about the mess tents and your fiction collapses like a wet tent. The look he gives you will outlast several of your organs.

text: Deep in the cave, a sky-metal pod stands upright, frosted glass fogged from inside. A panel blinks: 'OCCUPANT: 1. WAKE? Y/N.' Someone has scratched next to it: 'DONT'. Someone else has scratched: 'DO'.

outcomes.text: The pod hisses open. Inside: a very old, very annoyed tortoise wearing a tiny vest. It was NOT the original occupant. It bites you on principle and waddles off with centuries of secrets.
Empty. Long empty. But the occupant left their effects, and a note: 'gone to see the sky from the right side. take what you need.'
The pod BOOTS instead of opening, runs a diagnostic on YOU, and prints a receipt declaring you '87% within parameters.' You keep the receipt. It feels important.
The panel dims to a slow, sleeping pulse. 'MAYBE NEXT CENTURY,' it displays. You add your own scratch to the debate: 'NOT YET.'

success.text: Through the glass: nothing but a folded uniform and a child's drawing of a sun. You leave the pod alone and take the long way out, thinking about it.

fail.text: The glass fogs FROM YOUR SIDE as something inside exhales. You set a personal record for cave exit velocity.

text: A delegation of swamp folk has set up in the square, selling jars of gray-green paste and glaring at anyone who wrinkles their nose. Their banner reads, in dripping letters: 'IT IS GOOD FOR YOU.'

outcomes.text: It tastes like a pond's opinion of you. It is also, infuriatingly, good for you.
It tastes like a pond's opinion of you, and the pond was right. Still works though.

'Plague in town,' says the eldest delegate, and doubles the price with a look that dares you to complain. You pay it. The paste burns going down like a disinfectant with opinions, and for days afterward you are the healthiest thing in the district.
The eldest delegate looks at your purse, then your face, then pushes a jar across for a single coin. 'Eat,' she says, in the tone of every grandmother who ever lived. You do not argue with the tone.
The eldest delegate says a word with four consecutive glottal stops and offers no translation. The younger one mouths 'you don't want to know' with real kindness.
The glare you receive is generational. It carries the weight of every dried-out ancestor who was ever sneered at by city folk. You buy two jars out of pure shame.

text: The back wall of {site} is papered with wanted posters, some so old the faces have faded to smudges with bounties. Patrons play darts against the top row.

outcomes.text: You memorize three faces and two rewards. Knowledge like this either makes you money or makes you careful. Ideally both.

You study the top row the way a tradesman reads competitors' prices. Two of the bounties are higher than yours, which is somehow insulting. You memorize their mistakes, which in your line of work counts as free education.
There you are, top row, mid-dartboard. The artist gave you better cheekbones. You quietly take it down and pocket it. One less copy in the world.
There you are - and a patron looks from the poster to you, twice. You buy the whole bar a round with the speed of the truly motivated.
Triple bullseye into a smudge-faced outlaw from another era. The bar cheers. The house buys your next drink.
Your dart finds the wall, the doorframe, and very nearly the barkeep. You are handed a spoon and told it's spoon night.

text: A room of rotted shelves and one miraculously dry cabinet, stamped with the seal of a faction you half-recognize. Records survive in here. Records, and the smell of very old decisions.

outcomes.text: Tax rolls, troop counts, a love letter misfiled under 'GRAIN.' History from the inside, unglamorous and real.

success.text: You select the pages with names still worth blackmail... that is, HISTORICAL VALUE. Collectors will pay handsomely.

fail.text: You grab the impressive-looking ones. They turn out to be 300-year-old inventory lists for socks. Even the rats judge your curation.

choices.text: Burn it all. Some history should rest

outcomes.text: The cabinet goes up like a confession. Somewhere, several old grudges lose their evidence. The dungeon feels lighter. So do you.

text: An entire goblin warren is migrating: forty goblins, one road, furniture on backs, a grandfather clock carried by six of them like a fallen king. A scooter outrider waves you to the shoulder.

outcomes.text: You take a corner of the clock for three miles. Goblin moving songs are 90% complaints and 10% threats against stairs. At the rest stop they feed you gratitude stew, which is better than it sounds.
They hand you the jar shelf. THE JAR SHELF. You learn what's in goblin jars. You carry it anyway, forever changed.
'Landlord,' says the matriarch, and spits with impressive range. Nothing more needs saying. You spit too, in solidarity.
'The old warren WOKE UP,' says a small goblin, and is immediately shushed by four adults. You walk a little faster after that.

choices.text: Sell them supplies at migration prices

outcomes.text: Forty hungry goblins, one seller. You could gouge. You charge fair and they remember it - goblins keep excellent books on who gouges.

text: A hermit's hut with a shelf out front: 'TAKE A THING, LEAVE A THING. NO SHOUTING.' The current inventory: a boot, a jar of teeth, a small excellent painting of a duck, and a bell you are certain rang by itself.

outcomes.text: Rock for duck painting: the deal of the century. From inside the hut, a satisfied grunt. The duck follows you with its eyes in a comforting way.
You leave your second-best regret on the shelf in exchange (the shelf accepts abstractions). The bell rings when danger is near, or when it's bored. Same ring.
The bell SCREAMS. From the hut: 'NO SHOUTING!' You put it back. The bell smirks, audibly. Your nerves file a complaint.
Long silence. Then, under the door slides a card: 'no. but thank you for asking properly.' You have been declined with more warmth than most people greet you.

text: The food street: forty stalls, one alley, every vendor shouting. Skewers of what is PROBABLY chicken. A soup that glows. A pastry with a waiting list. Your stomach seizes executive control.

outcomes.text: The probably-chicken: excellent. The glowing soup: warm going down, luminous forever. The mystery skewer: best decision of your week.
Stall seven defeats you. Nobody gets through stall seven. You are carried to a bench by strangers who have all been there.
The glowing soup GLOWS BACK. You feel incredible. Strangers ask if you're okay. You've never been this okay.

Word of your purse travels the alley faster than you do. Vendors intercept you with their FINEST - the stuff under the counter, the skewer with a waiting list of its own. You eat like a magistrate and tip like a legend.
It's harvest season and the alley knows it: mushroom skewers, spiced squash, a mulled cider that has clearly been places. Even stall seven has mellowed. Autumn is when this street tells the truth.
Two hours in line. Worth it. You would testify to this under oath. You nearly weep into the crumbs.

choices.text: Just inhale the smells for free

outcomes.text: The vendors know your kind. One takes pity and hands you a broken skewer 'for quality control.' Dignity: negotiable. Skewer: delicious.

text: It's league night at {site}. A bracket board, folding chairs, a referee with a whistle and unshakable authority. Grundle's name is at the top, undefeated across three taverns. There's an open slot in the bracket.

success.text: You wrestle through three rounds to the Grundle semifinal, where you lose honorably and instantly. Third place pays actual money and eternal league standing.
GRUNDLE FORFEITS - a scheduling conflict with another bar's league. You win the bracket by default. The trophy is a bronzed elbow. You will treasure it always.

fail.text: Round one. Eight seconds. Your opponent apologizes WHILE winning, which is somehow worse. League rules entitle you to a consolation sandwich.

success.text: You set lines, take bets, and clean up when the favorite chokes in the quarters. The referee looks the other way for a modest consideration.

fail.text: You misprice Grundle (never misprice Grundle) and pay out to half the tavern. An expensive education in golem athletics.

outcomes.text: You call elbows all night with escalating confidence. By the final you have a whistle of your own and the referee calls you 'son' regardless of your age or relation.

text: A collapsed manor's cellar, racks intact. Bottles from before the wars, labels in a beautiful dead hand. One rack is chained and padlocked, which in your experience means either the best wine or the worst mistake.

outcomes.text: Six bottles of pre-war vintage. Collectors will duel over these. You wrap them in your spare shirt like the treasure they are.
The chained rack holds ONE bottle, unlabeled, humming faintly. You sell it to the weirdest merchant you know for the strangest money you've seen.
The bottle behind the chain is empty. The chain was to keep something IN the bottle. It isn't in the bottle anymore. It's had time to think about things.

choices.text: Drink a toast to the dead house

outcomes.text: You pour one glass, raise it to the fallen rafters, and drink. The vintage is extraordinary. For a moment the cellar feels like a dinner party instead of a grave.

Two glasses, raised to the fallen rafters. Your companion says a few words in a language you decide not to ask about, and for a moment the cellar has enough guests to count as a gathering. The dead house pours generously for company.

text: A wagon converted for pilgrims rattles past, gray-robed passengers packed in like devout cargo. It stops. A window slides open. 'ROOM FOR ONE,' intones the driver. 'DESTINATION: WHEREVER IT IS ALL GOING.'

outcomes.text: Four hours of chanting, shared bread, and a window seat on the world. They let you off at the next crossroads spiritually rinsed.
The chant has seventy verses and you learn all of them against your will. Weeks from now you will hum verse forty in a dungeon and feel inexplicably safe.

The wagon is standing room only - rain converts more pilgrims than any sermon. You wedge in among wet wool and shared bread, and the chant, with this many voices, does something to your sternum that you decline to examine.

choices.text: Ask where it is all going

outcomes.text: The driver looks at you for a long moment. 'Forward,' he says, and drives forward. Unfalsifiable. Deeply unsatisfying. You think about it for days.

success.text: You keep pace for a full mile while the pilgrims chant FASTER in encouragement. When you finally pull ahead, the whole wagon cheers. Blessed, sweaty victory.

fail.text: The wagon disappears over the hill to the sound of seventy voices harmonizing on the word 'slow.'

text: A cavern where echoes gather like moths. Traders in the know come here to sell SOUNDS: a dead singer's high note, a grandmother's laugh, the exact crunch of autumn. A blind broker sits at a stone desk, listening to inventory.

choices.text: Sell a sound of your own

outcomes.text: You give up the sound of your first door closing behind you. The broker pays well. You won't miss it, you think. You think.
You offer your best sigh. The broker auditions it, nods slowly: 'quality weariness.' Premium rate.
It comes in a shell. Hold it to your ear: October, forever. Some purchases are stupid and correct at the same time.

The broker plays you samples: rain on a tent, a crowd gasping, someone saying a name you almost recognize. 'Browsing is free,' he says. 'Feeling things costs extra.' He doesn't charge you. He should have.

text: A stall under a banner: 'BEAST ATTACK INSURANCE. COMPREHENSIVE. ACTS OF WYRM INCLUDED.' The actuary has a wooden leg, an eyepatch, and the serene confidence of someone whose premiums are working.

outcomes.text: You are now covered against mauling, hoarding-adjacent losses, and 'gaze-related petrification (partial).' The certificate is beautiful. The peace of mind is real, if actuarially dubious.
Reading the policy, you find clause 9: coverage void if you 'approach, taunt, or befriend' a beast. That's... that's your whole career. He will not refund you. Clause 12.

choices.text: Ask about the leg and the eyepatch

success.text: 'Uninsured,' he says simply, tapping the wood. The sales pitch of the century. You get a survivor's discount for listening and a genuinely useful map of lair territories.

fail.text: 'Shark,' he says flatly, in a city three weeks from any coast. You have offended an actuary. Your premium quote triples on the spot.

success.text: Your account of a 'diregoose incident' is so detailed, so vivid, that he pays out while openly admiring the craftsmanship. 'Come work for me,' he says. You take the payout instead.

fail.text: He asks which DIRECTION the diregoose attacked from. There is a correct answer. You do not know it. There is a fraud fee. You do pay it.

text: The forest opens onto old earthworks: a battlefield gone green, trenches soft with moss. A stone marker lists the fallen. Most names are weathered out. One is legible: {gone}.

success.text: Under the moss: buckles, a rusted blade-hilt, a purse the looters missed by inches and centuries. The forest kept it for whoever would look carefully.

fail.text: You find the one section of trench that still remembers how to collapse. The battlefield claims one more ankle, briefly.

outcomes.text: An hour with moss and grit under your nails. Four more names emerge into the light. Somewhere, four families' stories get their footnote back. Nobody pays you. It's the best work you do all week.

success.text: The quietest sleep you've had in weeks. Whatever's here doesn't mind company that asks permission first, which you did, out loud, feeling foolish. You woke up un-eaten. Not foolish.

fail.text: All night: marching. No feet, just the SOUND. You leave at dawn with gray hairs you intend to lie about.

text: Two cooks at {site} are dueling with ladles - actually dueling - over whose stew is canonical. The barkeep referees with visible exhaustion. Both stews simmer, and both cooks turn to YOU. 'Neutral tongue! JUDGE.'

success.text: You articulate the difference - one has depth, one has courage - and award a split decision so diplomatic both cooks claim victory. You eat free forever, in this one specific tavern.

fail.text: You pick a winner. The loser's ladle finds you at the speed of grief. The winner's gratitude does not cover medical.

choices.text: Declare both stews inferior to your grandmother's

outcomes.text: The cooks UNITE against a common enemy. They demand the recipe. You have no recipe. You have no grandmother of note. You improvise one from pure fear, and it's - it's actually good?
The cooks unite against a common enemy: your face. Two ladles. One lesson.

choices.text: Steal both recipes during the chaos

success.text: Two recipe cards, one pocket, zero witnesses. Sold separately to rival taverns in the next region, they fund a week of your life.

fail.text: Both cooks catch you mid-lift. The feud is OVER. The alliance is AGAINST YOU. The stews, you learn, are excellent projectiles.

choices.text: Enter your own jar of stew as a third contender

outcomes.text: You unstopper the jar with ceremony. Road stew: aged in a pack, seasoned with weather. Both cooks taste it, go pale, and unite to buy the recipe off you just to keep it out of the region.

Both cooks taste it and, for the first time in nine years, laugh at the same thing. The feud dissolves into pity. You are fed properly, as an act of charity, and your jar is quietly emptied into the pig trough.

text: The throne room. The throne. And ON the throne: a very comfortable goat. It has clearly been here for years. Around it, offerings - the dungeon's denizens have drawn their own conclusions about what sits on thrones.

choices.text: Pay respects to the goat king

outcomes.text: You bow. The goat regards you with rectangular-pupiled majesty and permits you to take from the offering pile. Wisest monarch you've ever dealt with. Lowest taxes, too.

You bow. The goat's rectangular pupils fix on something just over your shoulder - something that has been riding you for days. It walks over, chews the air beside your ear with terrible deliberation, and swallows. The curse is gone. The goat returns to the throne. Court is adjourned.

The goat's eyes go to your pack and stay there. It knows what you're carrying - things on thrones always do. It permits your tribute-taking, but as you leave, it bleats once, in what you will later realize was a warning.

success.text: The goat yields the throne with terrifying grace, as if it had already planned to abdicate TODAY, SPECIFICALLY. You sit. It's cold. The offerings stop immediately. The goat watches you learn.

fail.text: The headbutt carries the authority of an anointed sovereign. You are escorted from the throne room by momentum.

outcomes.text: You add your second-best item to the pile. The goat chews thoughtfully and nudges the BEST of the old offerings toward you with one hoof. Court favor, fairly traded.
